The -etvā suffix is the causative absolutive — 'having caused to stand, having established'. This is the final preparatory action before the main verb (sitting).

tips: Idha: 'Here' — referring to this teaching or context.

The -av- suffix is part of the stem for the vocative plural form of 'bhikkhu'.
The -e ending is vocative plural — calling out to a group: 'O mendicants!' Pāli marks the addressee on the noun itself.
Bhikkhu: 'mendicant, monk' — one who lives on alms. Same root as bhikkhave above, now in nominative singular.
√gam: 'to go' — the root appears here as 'ga' (weakened form in compounds).

The -ṁ ending is accusative singular — the direct object: 'cross-legged posture' (what is bent/folded).

√bhuj: 'to bend, fold' — related to 'bend', 'bow' in English. Here used for crossing the legs.

pana: and; moreover; on the other hand. Connecting particle.
assa: to/for him (gen/dat sg of 'ima'). 'panassa' = 'and/but for him'.
sati: mindfulness; presence. Nominative singular — the subject that 'paccupaṭṭhitā hoti'.

kiñci: anything (from kimañci). Indefinite pronoun, object of 'upādiyati'.
√lok: to perceive; loka = 'world'. Locative: 'in the world'.
upa- + ā-: upward or intensive; combined with √dā — 'to take up'.
Thematic vowel -a- + mediopassive marker -ya-: passive or reflexive — 'is clung to'.

phases.pali: ‘Atthi kāyo’ti vā panassa sati paccupaṭṭhitā hoti. Yāvadeva ñāṇamattāya paṭissatimattāya anissito ca viharati, na ca kiñci loke upādiyati.

tips: a-: Negative prefix — 'not'. Privation of the following: 'not dependent'.
√sri: To lean, depend — the root of 'relying on'. With ni- it means 'settled upon'.
Masculine nominative singular — 'he, independent'. The subject of viharati.

judge.rationale: The MODEL provides a correct analysis with additional detail on the grammatical role and root meaning, but it slightly misrepresents the core sense of 'not leaning on craving' as it does not explicitly mention craving.

tips: ca: And — coordinating conjunction. Links the two clauses: 'independent AND dwells'.

judge.rationale: The MODEL accurately captures the meaning and function of 'ca' as a coordinating conjunction without any errors.

tips: na: Not — the standard negation particle in Pāli.

judge.rationale: The MODEL correctly identifies 'na' as the standard negation particle in Pāli, matching the GOLDEN analysis.

tips: ca: And — same conjunction as p12. Pairs with na to mean 'and not'.

judge.rationale: The MODEL accurately reflects the meaning and function of 'ca' as a conjunction, consistent with the GOLDEN analysis.

tips: kiñ (root kim): What? — interrogative pronoun. With ci it becomes indefinite: 'anything'.
-ci: Indefinite suffix — makes kim into 'something, anything'.

judge.rationale: The MODEL provides a correct analysis with additional detail on the indefinite nature of 'kiñci', but it does not explicitly mention the context of negation with 'na'.

tips: √lok: To see, perceive — the root of 'world'. A 'world' is a domain of seeing/perceiving.
Locative singular — 'in the world'. The -e ending marks location: where the grasping would occur.

judge.rationale: The MODEL correctly identifies the locative singular but omits the essential point that 'loke' refers specifically to 'in the world' as an experiential context.

tips: Full stem: upa (final vowel elided in sandhi before ādi)
Opening quotation mark — marks the start of a direct thought/recollection phrase.
√as: To be, to exist — the verb of being. From this root come all 'is/are/exist' forms.
Third-person singular present active — 'there is'. The -i ending makes it present tense.
kāya: body — the physical body, accumulation of parts. The same word is used for 'group' or 'collection'.
Elision of 'iti' — a quotative marker meaning 'thus'. Here it closes the direct thought: 'The body exists, thus.'
Final -i of 'iti' — marks the end of the quotation.
vā: Or — an alternative particle. Here it introduces a new possibility: 'Or mindfulness is established...'
pana: But, moreover — a discourse particle shifting focus. It marks a transition in thought.
assa: For him — dative/genitive singular of the pronoun 'that one'. Indicates the meditator.
sati: mindfulness — from √smṛ 'to remember'. In Buddhism, it means presence of mind, awareness.
pati-: Toward, against — directional prefix. Here it suggests 'standing nearby' or 'present'.
upa-: Toward, near — intensifies the sense of closeness.
√sthā: To stand — the root of 'standing'. With prefixes, it means 'stood near' or 'attended'.
hoti: Is — third-person singular present of √bhū 'to be'. Used here as a copula.
yāva: As far as, until — a relative adverb of extent.
deva: indeed — emphatic particle. Together with yāva it means 'only as much as'.
euphonic -va: inserted for sandhi — no independent meaning.
ñāṇa: knowledge, gnosis — from √jñā 'to know'. Wisdom in a direct, experiential sense.
matta: measure, amount — from √mā 'to measure'. Suggests a limited, just-sufficient quantity.
Feminine dative singular — 'for a measure of knowledge'. The dative shows purpose.
pati-: Toward, back — directional prefix. Here it can mean 'continuous' or 'toward recollection'.
sati: mindfulness — the same stem as p5. Here with prefix paṭi-, it suggests 'recollected mindfulness'.
matta: measure — same stem as p9s2. 'Enough of mindfulness.'
vi-: Intensifier — 'apart, thoroughly'. Intensifies the root meaning 'to move about'.
√hṛ: To carry, take — the root of 'moving about'. With vi- it means 'to dwell, abide'.
Thematic vowel a — links the root to the personal ending.
√dā: To give, to take — the root of 'giving'. With ā- it takes the sense of 'taking up, grasping'.
ya: passive/reflexive suffix — makes the verb 'takes up for oneself / clings.'

phases.pali: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kurūsu viharati kammāsadhammaṁ nāma kurūnaṁ nigamo.

senses: name of a town in the Kuru country

judge.rationale: The model correctly identifies the segmentation and grammatical role but omits the specific cultural and historical context of the name and its etymological nuances.

tips: Links proper name to common noun

senses: name of the people of Kuru; Kurus

tips: The -naṁ ending means 'of the' — Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself

tips: "A town" — what we are talking about
Literally 'a going down to' — a market town
The town is the subject of the clause

judge.rationale: The model effectively conveys the meaning and grammatical role of 'nigamo', with a slight difference in wording but no loss of essential information.

phases.pali: “bhikkhavo”ti.

tips: ˈbhikkh-: from the root √bhikkh, 'to share' or 'to beg' — a religious mendicant, not a pauper; someone who lives on received alms as a spiritual practice.
The -vo ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group respectfully, like 'O monks!'

judge.rationale: The MODEL provides a correct segmentation, accurate etymology, and captures the essential meaning of the vocative plural, with only a minor difference in the segmentation format.

phases.pali: Bhagavā etadavoca:

tips: Fortune, good luck; from √bhaj: to share
The root here means 'one who has fortune'
The -vā ending is nominative singular of the possessive suffix -vant (like -mant)
One who possesses fortune — the Sublime One
Same stem as in phase-b and phase-e, now as the speaker/subject

judge.rationale: The MODEL provides a correct analysis with additional depth regarding the etymology and grammatical role, enhancing the understanding without introducing errors.

tips: Like the "e-" augment in the Greek aorist (Sanskrit uses "a-")
Sandhi glide consonant linking eta- and avoca
Prefix a-: a past-tense augment (like English 'did speak')

phases.pali: Katame cattāro?

tips: katama asks for selection from a known set
The -e ending marks masculine plural — asking about a group

tips: Nominative plural masculine form of catur (four)

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: "Body in body" = seeing body AS body
Kāya: body — literally 'an accumulation', the physical form.
The -e ending is locative singular — 'in the body' or 'in regard to the body'. English uses 'in/on' here; Pāli marks it on the noun.

judge.rationale: The MODEL provides a correct segmentation and accurate tooltips, capturing the essential meanings, though it could have included more nuances from the GOLDEN.

tips: Identity shift: you become an observer
Kāya: body — same stem as p4, now as the first member of a compound.
√paś: to see, to behold. With anu- prefix means 'observing after' or 'watching closely.'
The -ī ending marks a possessive adjective — 'one who observes the body'. Same pattern as ātāpī (p7).

judge.rationale: The MODEL accurately segments and explains the term but misses some key aspects of sustained observation and identity shift present in the GOLDEN.

tips: vi-: A prefix meaning 'apart, separate, intensively.' It adds force or distinction to the root.
√har: to take, to carry, to dwell. In Pāli, 'viharati' means 'dwells, abides, stays' — carrying oneself along in a state.
The thematic vowel -a- connects the root to the personal ending.
The -ti ending is third-person singular present — 'he/she dwells'. Descriptive, not past or future.

judge.rationale: The MODEL correctly identifies the segmentation and provides accurate meanings, although it could have been more concise in its explanation.

tips: Idha: Here, now — in this teaching, in this context.
The -e ending is vocative plural — calling out to a group of monks. Same stem as 'bhikkhu' (p3).
From √bhikkh: to share, to beg — a 'mendicant', one who lives on alms.
From √tap: to burn, to heat. In Buddhism, 'ardent' means with the burning energy of practice — the heat that consumes defilements.
The -ī ending marks a possessive adjective — 'one who has ardor'. Same suffix as in kāyānupassī (p5).
sam-: A prefix meaning 'together, completely.' Here it combines with pa- to intensify 'knowing'.
pa-: A prefix meaning 'forth, forward.' Together sam-pa- means 'fully, thoroughly'.
√jñā: to know. Sampajāna means 'clearly knowing' — active, continuous comprehension, not static knowledge.
From √smṛ: to remember. Sati is mindfulness — not just memory, but present-moment awareness.
The -mant suffix (contracted to -mā) means 'one who possesses' — 'one who has mindfulness'.
vi-: A prefix meaning 'apart, separate.' Here it suggests removal or setting aside.
√nī: to lead. With vi- prefix, 'vineyya' means 'having led away' — i.e., removed, set aside.
The -yya suffix marks an absolutive (gerund) — 'having removed'. It describes a completed action that sets the stage for what follows.
Loka: world, realm of beings — from √luc: to shine, to be bright; the visible world.
The -e ending is locative singular — 'in the world'. Same ending as kāye (p4).
abhijjhā: covetousness, greed — from abhi (toward) + √dhyai (to think). 'Thinking toward' = wanting.
domanassa: displeasure, aversion — from du (bad, difficult) + manas (mind). 'Bad-mindedness'.
The -ṁ ending is accusative singular neuter — the object of the removal: 'covetousness and displeasure'.
